briefing.headline: 12 high-priority items in the latest EM scan
briefing.summary: Top sources today: AJEM, EMJ, EMCrit. Main themes: trials, guideline or formal recommendation, reviews. Best early reads: 2025 guideline updates for community-acquired pneumonia diagnosis and management; Acute hyperkalaemia in emergency care: evidence-based approaches.
briefing.editorialHeadline: Guidelines tighten, shortcuts get challenged, and stroke systems keep evolving
briefing.editorialSummary:
Today's set is dominated by practical protocol work rather than shiny novelty. The biggest bedside updates are the 2025 CAP guideline refresh, a clean hyperkalaemia management review, and a pragmatic poisoning framework that reinforces structured toxidrome-first assessment without replacing poison-centre advice. At the same time, several papers push back on reflexive habits: pediatric septic shock fluid choice looks less decisive than many assumed, asymptomatic hypertension belongs mainly in outpatient pathways, lights-and-sirens do not appear to buy meaningful reperfusion time in stroke, and minor stroke thrombolysis still needs a disability-based rather than label-based approach.

The rest of the list is heavy on system-level decision support. Crashing PE care, thrombectomy routing, MeVO stroke intervention, ETT ultrasound confirmation, trauma prehospital timing, and chest-pain pretest probabilities all inform how aggressively we mobilise resources. Overall theme: use newer evidence to simplify high-risk algorithms, reserve escalation for patients who are likely to benefit, and be careful not to mistake process intensity for outcome benefit.

dailyEditorial.title: 2025 guideline updates for community-acquired pneumonia diagnosis and management
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For uncomplicated CAP, think stewardship first: confirm the diagnosis carefully, do not reflexively give antibiotics to every viral-positive patient, and when antibiotics are needed aim for the shortest evidence-supported course rather than the old default.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This CAP update matters because it trims management back toward evidence rather than habit.
dailyEditorial.summary: This CAP update matters because it trims management back toward evidence rather than habit. The most practice-relevant shifts are greater acceptance of clinician-performed lung ultrasound, narrower use of antibiotics when a convincing viral diagnosis is present, and shorter treatment courses for nonsevere disease.

dailyEditorial.title: Acute hyperkalaemia in emergency care: evidence-based approaches
dailyEditorial.takeaway: At the bedside, calcium treats electrical instability, not the potassium number. Pair insulin-dextrose with inhaled beta-agonist early, look hard for dialysis candidates, and do not lean on sodium polystyrene as if it solves the acute problem.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This review is a useful reset on acute hyperkalaemia: stabilise the myocardium when indicated, shift potassium quickly, and do not confuse temporising therapy with true elimination.
dailyEditorial.summary: This review is a useful reset on acute hyperkalaemia: stabilise the myocardium when indicated, shift potassium quickly, and do not confuse temporising therapy with true elimination. It also usefully downranks legacy treatments with poor evidence or meaningful harms.

dailyEditorial.title: EMCrit 424 – 2026 Crashing PE Update
dailyEditorial.takeaway: If the PE patient is crashing, move quickly from classification to physiology: RV failure, obstructive shock burden, bleeding risk, and local access to lysis, thrombectomy, or ECMO should drive the plan.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This update is most valuable as a synthesis piece: it aligns new AHA language with an operational bedside pathway for the patient who is actively deteriorating from PE.
dailyEditorial.summary: This update is most valuable as a synthesis piece: it aligns new AHA language with an operational bedside pathway for the patient who is actively deteriorating from PE. That makes it more useful for real resuscitation than another abstract taxonomy discussion.

dailyEditorial.title: Management of patients with suspected but unidentified poisoning in the emergency department: a joint Royal College of Emergency Medicine and National Poisons Information Service best practice guideline
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Treat the toxidrome and trajectory first. Stabilise, identify likely agent classes, think about delayed deterioration, and then use TOXBASE/poisons support for the poison-specific moves.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This joint RCEM-NPIS guideline gives a strong general frame for the poisoned patient when the agent is not yet known.
dailyEditorial.summary: This joint RCEM-NPIS guideline gives a strong general frame for the poisoned patient when the agent is not yet known. The practical value is in forcing clinicians back to syndromic thinking, anticipated toxicity progression, and repeated reassessment rather than premature diagnostic certainty.

dailyEditorial.title: PRoMPT BOLUS: A Landmark PECARN Trial Defining Fluid Choice in Pediatric Sepsis
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Do not let fluid brand distract from early recognition, antibiotics, vasoactive support, and reassessment. Either saline or balanced crystalloid is a defensible initial choice based on this trial.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: PRoMPT BOLUS is helpful largely because it shrinks a long-running argument.
dailyEditorial.summary: PRoMPT BOLUS is helpful largely because it shrinks a long-running argument. In pediatric septic shock, balanced fluid versus 0.9% saline did not separate on major clinical outcomes, which makes fluid choice less of a heroic decision than overall resuscitation quality.

dailyEditorial.title: Mothership and Drip-and-Ship Strategies in Mechanical Thrombectomy for Acute Ischemic Stroke
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For suspected LVO, the right pathway depends on local travel times, transfer friction, and thrombolysis access. Optimise the regional system rather than assuming mothership or drip-and-ship is always superior.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This meta-analysis addresses a systems question every stroke network wrestles with: direct-to-thrombectomy centre versus stop-first-transfer-later.
dailyEditorial.summary: This meta-analysis addresses a systems question every stroke network wrestles with: direct-to-thrombectomy centre versus stop-first-transfer-later. The paper is useful less as a universal answer and more as a reminder that routing strategy is a time-and-geography problem, not a slogan.

dailyEditorial.title: Minor Stroke, Major Uncertainty
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Reserve IV thrombolysis for patients with minor stroke symptoms only when the functional deficit is real and clinically important. Avoid treating the label when the patient is neurologically trivial.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The message here is appropriately conservative: “minor stroke” is not by itself a treatment decision, but nor is it a reason to give thrombolysis automatically.
dailyEditorial.summary: The message here is appropriately conservative: “minor stroke” is not by itself a treatment decision, but nor is it a reason to give thrombolysis automatically. What matters is whether the deficit is genuinely disabling and measurable.

dailyEditorial.title: Refining Early Rule-Out Strategies for Acute Myocardial Infarction: Insights and Opportunities From the LEGEND Trial
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For low-risk possible ACS, a single troponin pathway may support earlier discharge, but only when paired with sound pretest assessment, ECG review, and explicit follow-up/safety-netting.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The LEGEND discussion reinforces a familiar direction in chest-pain care: a well-chosen single hs-troponin strategy can safely accelerate rule-out when embedded in a broader decision framework.
dailyEditorial.summary: The LEGEND discussion reinforces a familiar direction in chest-pain care: a well-chosen single hs-troponin strategy can safely accelerate rule-out when embedded in a broader decision framework. The extra nuance is the role of shared decision-making rather than lab value absolutism.

dailyEditorial.title: LIBERATE-D – Conservative Dialysis Strategy
dailyEditorial.takeaway: If the AKI-D patient is clinically stable, ask whether dialysis is needed now rather than whether it is “due.” Volume status, potassium, acidosis, and uraemic complications should drive treatment.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: LIBERATE-D is a worthwhile challenge to routine scheduling behaviour in stable AKI-D.
dailyEditorial.summary: LIBERATE-D is a worthwhile challenge to routine scheduling behaviour in stable AKI-D. The interesting signal is that an indication-triggered approach may allow more renal recovery instead of locking patients into dialysis momentum.

dailyEditorial.title: New Guidelines Emphasize Outpatient Treatment of Asymptomatic Hypertension
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Avoid reflex IV treatment or rapid BP correction in the asymptomatic patient. Focus on confirming no hypertensive emergency, arranging follow-up, and starting oral therapy only when outpatient continuity is realistic.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: These guidelines continue the drift away from aggressive ED treatment of asymptomatic hypertension without end-organ injury.
dailyEditorial.summary: These guidelines continue the drift away from aggressive ED treatment of asymptomatic hypertension without end-organ injury. The meaningful decision is no longer “can I lower the number?” but “can this patient reliably enter outpatient care?”

dailyEditorial.title: RENOVATE or an Exercise in Statistical Futility? The Question of Flow or Pressure for Acute Hypoxic Respiratory Failure
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Choose HFNO or NIV based on work of breathing, recruitability, tolerance, and the underlying process. Reassess early and be ready to intubate rather than defending a modality too long.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: RENOVATE adds more reason to avoid rigid tribalism between HFNO and NIV in hypoxic respiratory failure.
dailyEditorial.summary: RENOVATE adds more reason to avoid rigid tribalism between HFNO and NIV in hypoxic respiratory failure. The practical lesson is that device choice alone is unlikely to rescue poor patient selection or delayed escalation.

dailyEditorial.title: Alternate Defibrillation Strategies for Refractory Ventricular Fibrillation
dailyEditorial.takeaway: For refractory VF, vector change or DSED remain reasonable escalation concepts where protocols and training exist, but standard high-quality CPR and minimising delays between shocks still matter most.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This secondary DOSE-VF analysis keeps alternate defibrillation strategies in play, especially because reduced VF burden may be one mechanism behind outcome differences.
dailyEditorial.summary: This secondary DOSE-VF analysis keeps alternate defibrillation strategies in play, especially because reduced VF burden may be one mechanism behind outcome differences. It is intriguing, but still not the same as proving an early universal protocol change.

dailyEditorial.title: Endovascular Thrombectomy Boosts Outcomes in Medium-Vessel Ischemic Stroke
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Do not dismiss medium-vessel occlusion as automatically “too distal.” If deficits are significant and the system can move quickly, discuss thrombectomy candidacy early with your stroke team.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The ORIENTAL-MeVO signal is important because MeVO stroke has been a more ambiguous thrombectomy space than proximal LVO.
dailyEditorial.summary: The ORIENTAL-MeVO signal is important because MeVO stroke has been a more ambiguous thrombectomy space than proximal LVO. This report supports the idea that selected patients with meaningful deficits may benefit from intervention rather than default medical management.

dailyEditorial.title: Comparison of three point-of-care ultrasound techniques to confirm endotracheal tube placement: A randomized clinical trial
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Use bedside ultrasound as a real adjunct to ETT confirmation, especially when capnography is delayed or the physiology is messy. Pick the technique your team can perform fastest without sacrificing reliability.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This trial is clinically useful because it moves beyond “ultrasound works” and asks which confirmation technique is fastest.
dailyEditorial.summary: This trial is clinically useful because it moves beyond “ultrasound works” and asks which confirmation technique is fastest. All three approaches performed well, so the operational win is choosing a method your airway team can execute immediately and reproducibly.

dailyEditorial.title: Association between prehospital time and trauma outcomes: A multicenter cohort study from the Pan-Asian Trauma Outcomes Study (PATOS)
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Shorter is not automatically better if critical interventions are being delivered. Focus on eliminating unnecessary delay while recognising that the sickest trauma patients often require more on-scene work.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: PATOS again shows the tension in prehospital trauma metrics: longer scene or transport intervals correlate with worse outcomes, but part of that signal reflects case severity rather than pure inefficiency.
dailyEditorial.summary: PATOS again shows the tension in prehospital trauma metrics: longer scene or transport intervals correlate with worse outcomes, but part of that signal reflects case severity rather than pure inefficiency. It is a systems-improvement paper, not a simplistic stopwatch mandate.

dailyEditorial.title: Association between initial bandemia degree and short-term mortality among adults with bacteremia in the emergency department
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Marked bandemia in bacteremia should raise your concern for bad trajectory and support tighter reassessment, earlier escalation, and attention to source control—but do not manage the differential count in isolation.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This cohort suggests that the degree of bandemia carries additional prognostic information in ED bacteremia.
dailyEditorial.summary: This cohort suggests that the degree of bandemia carries additional prognostic information in ED bacteremia. The finding is clinically plausible, but it should be used as a severity nudge rather than elevated into a stand-alone decision rule.

dailyEditorial.title: Eye-related emergency calls and prehospital management in region Zealand, Danmark: a register-based cohort study
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Acute visual complaints need a neurovascular lens as well as an ocular one. Sudden monocular or field-type symptoms should trigger at least a brief stroke-oriented history and exam pathway.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: The standout point here is not the expected mix of chemical injuries, vision loss, and trauma; it is the stroke contamination hidden inside “eye complaint” dispatch buckets.
dailyEditorial.summary: The standout point here is not the expected mix of chemical injuries, vision loss, and trauma; it is the stroke contamination hidden inside “eye complaint” dispatch buckets. That is a meaningful prehospital diagnostic blind spot.

dailyEditorial.title: Prehospital Use of Lights and Sirens in Stroke Is Associated With Faster Door-to-CT Times but Not Door-to-Thrombolysis or Door-to-Endovascular Therapy Times
dailyEditorial.takeaway: If you want faster stroke treatment, invest in prenotification, destination decisions, and in-hospital activation. Lights and sirens alone are not the lever that fixes reperfusion delay.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This study is a good antidote to performative urgency.
dailyEditorial.summary: This study is a good antidote to performative urgency. Lights and sirens may speed CT arrival a little, but they did not shorten times to thrombolysis or thrombectomy and did not improve outcomes.

dailyEditorial.title: The prevalence of life-threatening diagnoses in emergency department patients with chest pain in a national group
dailyEditorial.takeaway: Use chest-pain probabilities to prioritise ACS first, then PE, pneumothorax, and aortic disease according to the story and exam. Broad shotgun workup is less useful than disciplined pretest reasoning.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This prevalence paper is useful because chest-pain workups often suffer from vague pretest thinking.
dailyEditorial.summary: This prevalence paper is useful because chest-pain workups often suffer from vague pretest thinking. Knowing that roughly 1 in 18 atraumatic chest-pain presentations hide a life-threatening diagnosis helps anchor risk without imagining every patient has an exotic catastrophe.

dailyEditorial.title: Etiology-specific predictors for short-term functional outcomes of OHCA: A Japanese Nationwide registry study
dailyEditorial.takeaway: When arrest likely stems from catastrophic neurologic pathology, be careful applying generic OHCA expectations. Early prognostic thinking should weigh the suspected cause and initial neurologic injury burden, not just resuscitation interval metrics.
dailyEditorial.shortSummary: This registry study argues that not all OHCA prognostication obeys the same rules.
dailyEditorial.summary: This registry study argues that not all OHCA prognostication obeys the same rules. In cerebrovascular-origin arrest, the burden of primary brain injury may dominate outcome more than the timing variables that matter in other arrest phenotypes.
